text:
On Novig, a parlay is matched peer-to-peer — you're betting against other users, not a house. Each leg of your parlay gets matched separately against different counterparties who've offered odds on that same event.

The price on each leg comes from what other users are willing to offer. When you place a parlay order, our system looks for users on the other side willing to take those odds for each leg. It's similar to how a single bet works on an exchange — the difference is you're stringing multiple legs together, and each one has to find its own match.

You don't need a single counterparty to take all the legs — the system matches each leg independently. If all legs get matched, your parlay is live. If any leg doesn't find a match at your requested odds, that leg stays unmatched until someone takes it or you adjust.

For the exact mechanics of how legs are prioritized or matched when multiple orders are in the queue, a human on our team can walk you through it in more detail — send that over if you want the specifics.

Novig Support
